attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of intensely itchy skin lesions on her arms and legs. She denies previous similar rashes. She has no other medical history and takes the combined oral contraceptive pill for contraception. She denies any new chemicals or changes in her occupation as a baker. She discloses she has had over ten casual sexual partners this year and does not use condoms. She lives with 2 housemates in the city.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. There are multiple symmetrically distributed, excoriated papules on her extremities with areas of post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation. They do not involve her mucous membranes, web spaces, palms or soles.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with characteristic features of papular pruritic eruption (PPE), a common cutaneous manifestation of HIV infection.

Papular pruritic eruption typically presents with symmetrically distributed, intensely pruritic papules predominantly affecting the extremities. The presence of excoriations and post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation with a history of multiple unprotected sexual encounters supports this diagnosis.

Scabies typically presents with intense nocturnal pruritus and burrows in characteristic locations such as between fingers, wrists, and genitals. While it can be more severe in HIV-positive patients, the symmetrical distribution and chronic nature of the lesions in this case are more consistent with PPE. PPE also does not tend to involve the web spaces which would be involved in scabies.

Folliculitis presents with inflammatory papules or pustules centred on hair follicles. While it can occur in HIV-positive patients, the distribution pattern and chronic nature of the lesions make this diagnosis less likely.

Atopic dermatitis typically presents with poorly defined erythematous patches and plaques with scaling, often in flexural areas. The discrete papular nature of the lesions in this case makes atopic dermatitis less likely.

Insect bites typically present as acute papules with central puncta and tend to be grouped in exposed areas. The chronic nature and symmetric distribution of the lesions make insect bites less likely.

PPE is a stage 2 HIV disease and may be the presenting symptom in 25 - 70% of patients. Treatment typically involves confirmation of HIV infection, viral load and CD4 lymphocyte count (typically less than 200x109/L), antihistamines, topical steroids, and most importantly then optimising HIV management.
learningPoints: Papular pruritic eruption is characterised by symmetrical, intensely pruritic papules predominantly affecting the extremities in people with HIV.
The presence of excoriated papules, post-inflammatory
Papular pruritic eruption is a marker of HIV disease progression and may indicate the need to start or adjust antiretroviral therapy.
references: Tallon B.  Pruritic papular eruption of HIV.  DermNet NZ.  2021.  Available at: https://dermnetnz.org/topics/pruritic-papular-eruption-of-hiv

Mohseni Afshar Z, Goodarzi A, Emadi SN, Miladi R, Shakoei S, Janbakhsh A, Aryanian Z, Hatami P. A Comprehensive Review on HIV-Associated Dermatologic Manifestations: From Epidemiology to Clinical Management. Int J Microbiol. 2023  Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10368516/#sec3
